metadata.description: Find and correct your online exposure.

common.riskStamp: {risk, select, high {high risk} medium {medium risk} low {low risk} other {{risk} risk}}
common.untranslated: Not yet available in this language — shown in its original language.

appBar.tagline: a published list of corrections · no account · no server

panic.label: Clear the desk
panic.aria: Clear the desk: instantly delete all Errata data on this device and reload
panic.title: Instantly delete all data on this device and reload

footer.note: Everything stays on your device. No account, no servers, no tracking.

landing.heroAria: The record says the old entry — corrected to your name.
landing.heroSupport: You are not scrubbing a secret. You are correcting a publishing error — with the authority of the person the record is about. Errata drafts every correction; you press send.
landing.jumpLead: You’re set up. Jump straight to a step:
landing.jumpHardenLead: Want to clean up your accounts?
landing.jumpHarden: Harden platforms & remove your former name →
landing.jumpRecordsLead: Deadname in court records or archives?
landing.dropKicker: THE HERO PATHS · ONE REQUEST GOES FAR
landing.dropBody: In California, the Delete Act's DROP orders every registered data broker to delete your record in a single request. In Colombia, habeas data (Ley 1581) obliges any data holder to act on your deletion claim within 15 business days. Errata prepares both — you press send.
landing.footnote1: Every search and draft is prepared in this tab; Errata has no server to send it to.
landing.footnote2: “Clear the desk” wipes local data instantly. It is always one click away, on every screen.

discover.title: Discover
discover.marginLedger: the ledger below keeps your place if you stop here
discover.intro: Find what’s out there the way someone looking for you would. Go at your own pace — you can stop and come back.
discover.marginCopy: your former name is copied when you ask, never shown on screen
discover.next: When you’re ready to act on what you found, <link>prepare removal requests →</link>

remediate.title: Remediate
remediate.intro: For each broker, Errata prepares a removal request from your details. Read it, decide which name the listing is under, then send it yourself.
remediate.ephemeralNote: Most people need more than one sitting for this — saving keeps your tracker for next time.
remediate.marginDrafted: each request is drafted on this device — nothing sends until you press send
remediate.paywallNote: Couldn’t confirm a listing because the site wanted payment or a sign-up? Send the request anyway — brokers have to process it whether or not you saw the report. Each request carries only the one name you choose below, so you’re not handing them anything new.
remediate.noFindingsTip: These are the brokers Errata covers. Tip: <link>run Discover</link> first to focus on the ones you’re actually listed on.
remediate.onlyFlagged: Show only brokers from my Discover findings
remediate.nextHarden: Listed on platforms too? <link>Harden your accounts & remove your former name →</link>
remediate.nextRecords: Deadname in a court order, name-change petition, or web archive? <link>Tackle public records →</link>

harden.title: Harden platforms
harden.intro: Update your accounts so they carry your current name, and lock down what others can see. Each platform leads with removing your former name, then hardening. Go at your own pace — the easiest wins are first.
harden.nextRecords: The most permanent sources — court orders, name changes, archives — <link>are handled here →</link>

records.title: Public records & name change
records.intro: These are the hardest, most permanent deadname sources — the indexed court order, the name-change petition’s publication, and the archives and caches that keep old copies alive. Some can be sealed or removed; some can only be monitored. Errata is honest about which.
records.banner: Informational only — not legal advice, and not yet reviewed by a lawyer. Name-change and record-sealing rules vary by state and county and change often; verify with your court or a legal-aid org before acting.
records.stateNote: Pick your state to see state-specific court-record guidance. Archive and cache removals work regardless of where you live.
records.coNote: Colombia's guidance is national — name change and the sex-marker correction go through a notary, not a public court case. Archive and cache removals work regardless of where you live.

playbook.title: Your deadname-removal playbook
playbook.intro: The whole path in one place, in the order that tends to work best. Do it at your own pace.
playbook.marginNote: this only reflects what is already saved on your device
playbook.deviceOnly: This reflects only what is saved on this device.
playbook.stageDiscoverBlurb: Search brokers, search engines, and platforms the way someone looking for you would — for your current name and your former name. Build a private findings list.
playbook.stageDiscoverNone: Not started — find your exposure first.
playbook.stageBrokersBlurb: For each broker, Errata prepares a removal request on this device — you decide which name the listing is under and whether to include your other name, then send it yourself.
playbook.stageBrokersTracked: {count, plural, one {# broker site} other {# broker sites}} tracked
playbook.stageBrokersNone: Prepare opt-out requests for where you were found.
playbook.stagePlatformsBlurb: Update your accounts to your current name and lock down who can see what — Google, Instagram, X, LinkedIn, TikTok, Reddit. Each leads with removing your former name.
playbook.stagePlatformsTracked: {count, plural, one {# platform} other {# platforms}} hardened
playbook.stagePlatformsNone: Clean up the accounts that still carry your former name.
playbook.stageRecordsBlurb: The most permanent sources: the indexed court order, the name-change petition’s publication (and sealed-petition routes), web archives, and search caches.
playbook.stageRecordsTracked: {count, plural, one {# former-name removal} other {# former-name removals}} tracked
playbook.stageRecordsNone: Address the records that outlast everything else.
playbook.backupNext: Working over several sittings? <link>Export an encrypted backup →</link> so you can pick up where you left off, even if this browser clears its storage.
playbook.disclaimer: Errata is information, not legal advice. Rights and removal processes vary by state and change over time — verify before you rely on anything here.

howItWorks.metaDescription: Everything stays on your device. No account, no servers, nothing sent about you.
howItWorks.title: How Errata works
howItWorks.intro: Errata helps you find what’s published about you and correct it — without anyone holding your data. Here’s exactly how that works, in plain terms, so you can decide whether to trust it.
howItWorks.deviceBody: Errata runs entirely in your browser. There’s no account to create and nothing to sign up for. The record you build, the corrections you draft, and your progress are kept in this browser’s own storage, on this device. They’re never sent to us — because there is no “us” to send them to. Errata has no server that stores your data.
howItWorks.never1: No accounts, no login, no password.
howItWorks.never2: Never asks for your email address or phone number.
howItWorks.never3: No analytics, no trackers, no “engagement” metrics — not even the privacy-friendly kind.
howItWorks.never4: No third-party scripts. Nothing on the page phones home.
howItWorks.oneThingHead: The one thing that leaves your browser — and where it goes
howItWorks.oneThingBody: Errata does most of the work of a correction: it drafts the opt-out request, the letter, the form text. You press send. When you do, that request goes straight from your browser to the broker or platform you’re contacting — an email you send, a form you submit, text you paste. It doesn’t pass through Errata. That last keystroke is yours, and it’s the whole privacy guarantee: your name only ever moves when you decide to move it.
howItWorks.savedHead: Saved, or not saved — your choice
howItWorks.savedBody1: When you first open Errata, nothing is written to this device. You start in session-only mode: everything disappears when you close the tab. That’s the safe default for a shared, borrowed, or monitored device.
howItWorks.savedBody2: On your own private device, you can choose to save your progress so it’s here when you come back. You can switch modes anytime in <link>Settings</link>.
howItWorks.clearHead: “Clear the desk” is always one tap away
howItWorks.clearBody1: The button at the top of every screen wipes Errata’s data from this device instantly — the whole ledger, every draft, your settings.
howItWorks.clearBody2: Being honest about its reach: it clears what Errata stored. It can’t erase your browser history, a backup you’ve already downloaded, or the fact that the site was visited. On a shared device, a private or incognito window plus session-only mode is the stronger cover.
howItWorks.cantHead: What Errata can’t protect you from
howItWorks.cantIntro: We’d rather tell you the edges than oversell.
howItWorks.cant1: Someone with access to your unlocked device can see what’s on the screen. Session-only mode and a private window are your defenses here.
howItWorks.cant2: Your internet provider, and whoever hosts the site, can see that a device visited Errata — an IP address, the same as with any website. They can’t see what you did inside it. Running Errata offline or self-hosted removes even this.
howItWorks.cant3: A backup you export is a copy of your data on your disk. Errata encrypts backups by default; keep the passphrase somewhere safe, and remember that Downloads folders often sync to the cloud.
howItWorks.openHead: You don’t have to take our word for it
howItWorks.openBody1: Errata is open source. Anyone can read every line, check that it does what this page says, and run their own copy — offline, or on their own server, with no connection to us at all. If you know how, open your browser’s network tools while you use it: you’ll see it isn’t sending your information anywhere.
howItWorks.legalHead: A note on the legal information
howItWorks.legalBody: Where Errata explains your rights or a court process, that’s general information to help you get oriented — not legal advice, and laws change. For your own situation, a name-change legal-aid group or a law-school clinic is the right next step.
howItWorks.supportIntro: You don’t have to do this alone. These are outside organizations, not part of Errata:
howItWorks.supportTransLifeline: Trans Lifeline — peer support by and for trans people. US: 877-565-8860 · translifeline.org
howItWorks.supportAccessNow: Access Now Digital Security Helpline — free, confidential help for people facing digital threats. accessnow.org/help

settings.title: Settings
settings.panicNote: The red “Delete everything” button at the top of every screen wipes Errata’s data on this device instantly. It can’t reach your browser history, an already-downloaded backup, or the fact that the site was visited — export first if you want a copy you can restore.

safetyIntro.title: Before you start
safetyIntro.intro: Errata keeps everything on this device — no account, no servers, nothing sent about you. Right now nothing is being saved: you’re in session-only mode, and it all disappears when you close the tab.
safetyIntro.li1: On a shared or monitored device, stay in session-only mode — nothing is written to this device, so there’s nothing for anyone else to find.
safetyIntro.li2: On your own private device, you can save your progress so it’s here when you come back.
safetyIntro.li3: Clear the desk is always in the top corner. It wipes Errata from this device instantly. (It can’t erase your browser history or anything you’ve downloaded.)
safetyIntro.li4: You set the pace. You can stop and come back any time.
safetyIntro.notDurable: This browser isn’t saving data anyway (private mode or storage is blocked), so you’re already in session-only mode.
safetyIntro.save: Save my progress on this device

storageMode.ephemeralStatus: Session-only mode — nothing is saved to this device.
storageMode.confirmWipe: Switch to session-only and delete what’s already saved on this device?

nameInputs.note: We use these to build search strings for you. On a shared device, use a private / incognito window so the browser itself doesn’t remember what you type.

optOutInputs.note: We use these to fill in your requests. Your former name is only written into a request if you tick the box on that broker — by default it’s left out.

addFinding.hint: You don’t need to type your former name here — tick the box below and it’s recorded without writing the name down.

ledger.title: Your ledger
ledger.empty: Nothing here yet. As you work through the steps, add what you find.
ledger.deadnameLead: <hl>{count} expose your former name</hl> — those lead the list.

checklist.fillDetails: Fill in your details above and your search strings appear here.
checklist.privacyNote: Safest is to copy a query and paste it into a private / incognito window. “Run” opens the search in a new tab — it’s sent to the search engine and shows up in your browsing history. Searches for your former name always open in DuckDuckGo, which doesn’t profile you.

optout.subject: Subject
optout.formFallback: If the form errors or won’t show your listing, email them directly instead — the request works either way.
optout.paradoxHead: Opting out here can reveal the link itself.
optout.paradoxDefault: Submitting this request may tell the broker your current and former names are the same person.
optout.paradoxLeave: Leaving a low-reach listing alone and monitoring it is a legitimate choice — not a failure.
optout.requiresId: This broker asks for ID. Send only what is strictly required — redact everything else, and never include more than they ask for.
optout.listedUnderLegend: Which name is this listing filed under?
optout.listedBoth: Both names — I found separate listings
optout.formerWarning: This request will contain your former name, because the listing is filed under it — that is necessary to ask for this specific record’s removal. Your current name stays out unless you add it below.
optout.includeOther: Also include my {other, select, former {former name} current {current name} other {other name}} in this request
optout.includeOtherWarn: — off by default; adding it discloses the link
optout.includeStatusOn: Your {other, select, former {former name} current {current name} other {other name}} will be included in the {broker} request.
optout.includeStatusOff: Your {other, select, former {former name} current {current name} other {other name}} is left out of the {broker} request.
optout.missingPrimary: Add your {which, select, former {former name} current {current name} other {name}} in “Your details” above so Errata can fill this request in.
optout.bothWarning: You’ll send two separate requests — one per listing, each carrying only that listing’s name. Send them as two separate messages, ideally not back-to-back, so the broker can’t pair them up.
optout.bothSharedEmail: Both requests would carry the same reply-to email — that alone links your names for this broker. Use a different address for the former-name request, or send one of the two through the web form instead.
optout.bothSenderLinks: If you email both, the sender address links your names all by itself. Send them from two different addresses, or send one of the two through the web form instead.
optout.bothMissing: Add your {which, select, both {current and former names} current {current name} former {former name} other {names}} in “Your details” above so Errata can fill both requests in.

network.coversSingle: One request covers: <members></members>. Tracking it marks every site sent.
network.coversBackbone: One request to this family’s privacy contact asks for removal from all of them, but only {rep}’s own removal is verified. Tracking it marks {rep} sent and adds as re-check to-dos.

quicksend.summary: Quick pass — email every target in one sitting
quicksend.note: The same removal request the cards below prepare, one per target, keyed on your current name only — your former name is never included here. Found a listing under your former name? Use that broker’s card below, where including it is an explicit choice.
quicksend.missingName: Add your current name in “Your details” above and the requests fill in here.
quicksend.formOnly: {names} {count, plural, one {takes} other {take}} a web form only — use {count, plural, one {its card} other {their cards}} below.

tracker.title: Your tracker
tracker.empty: Nothing tracked yet. When you send an opt-out, add it here so you remember to re-check — brokers often re-list.
tracker.headlineCounts: {corrected} corrected, {waiting} waiting on replies, {draft} to draft —
tracker.headlineAttention: {count, plural, one {# needs} other {# need}} a follow-up now, below.
tracker.mixedOption: Mixed — pick one to set all

rights.title: Your rights where you live
rights.banner: Informational only — not legal advice, and not yet reviewed by a lawyer. Deletion rights vary by place and change often; verify your own before relying on any wording here.
rights.noneSelected: No state selected; showing the national baseline only.
rights.pickPrompt: Pick your state to see the deletion rights you can use. You don’t need to — the broker opt-outs below work regardless — but in some states the law gives you much stronger tools.
rights.noGuidance: We don’t have verified deletion-rights guidance for {state} yet. Your state may still grant rights we haven’t written up — and the broker opt-outs below work regardless of where you live.
rights.agent: You can appoint an authorized agent to make these requests for you.

jurisdiction.noneSelected: No state selected; showing national and global guidance only.
jurisdiction.coUnreviewed: The Colombia guidance is newly authored and has not yet had local expert review. Verify each step against the official source (sic.gov.co) before relying on it.

recordGuide.sealedNote: Sealed / confidential petitions: some states let people at risk change their name without it becoming a public record — ask your court or a legal-aid org before you file, since it’s far harder to undo afterward.

platformGuide.effortStamp: {difficulty, select, high {high effort} medium {medium effort} low {low effort} other {{difficulty} effort}}
platformGuide.noteIndirect: This platform can’t change that directly — but these steps still reduce what’s exposed.
platformGuide.verifiedNote: Steps last verified {date}. Platform settings move often — if a step looks different, follow the closest equivalent and let us know.

backup.title: Backup & restore
backup.intro: Your data lives only on this device. A backup is the durable copy you control — and on some browsers (notably iOS Safari) local data is cleared after about a week of not visiting, so an occasional export is the safe way to “resume later.”
backup.exportWarn: A backup can contain your former name. Encrypting it is the default for a reason — a downloaded file often syncs to iCloud or Google Drive.
backup.passphraseHint: You’ll need this exact passphrase to restore. There’s no recovery if you lose it.
backup.noPassphraseWarn: — not recommended; the file will be readable by anyone
backup.needPassphrase: Enter a passphrase, or choose to export without one.
backup.exportedEncrypted: Encrypted backup downloaded. Keep the passphrase somewhere safe — without it the file can’t be opened.
backup.exportedPlain: Plaintext backup downloaded. Anyone who opens this file can read your data, including your former name.
backup.exportFailed: Couldn’t create the backup. Try again.
backup.importedReplace: Backup restored — it replaced what was here.
backup.importedMerge: Backup merged into your current data.

copy.announceFailed: Copying is blocked here. Select the text above and copy it manually.

reportBroken.open: Something here looks out of date?
reportBroken.hint: The guide name and version are already attached, so you can leave this blank. If you do write something, never include your name, deadname, address, or anything personal — it will be posted publicly.
reportBroken.placeholder: e.g. the opt-out link 404s, or step 3 no longer matches the page
reportBroken.publicNote: This opens a public issue on GitHub, in a new tab, under your own GitHub account — anyone can see it and it stays public.
